narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 43.6% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 and LSAT score of 32, both notably below the averages of 3.24 and 37.27 seen among students who passed the bar. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3 also played a key role, sitting well under the typical 5.91 for successful bar takers. Among the factors the model weighed most heavily, your LSAT score stood out as the strongest positive signal, suggesting it helps pull your odds toward passing despite other concerns. Next in influence was your Year 3 decile, which pushed the prediction more toward failure risk, reflecting how it lags behind those of admitted students who typically perform higher in their classes. Your middle family income bracket provided a modest counterbalance, aligning with about a third of bar passers and slightly favoring success in the model's view. Compared to typical accepted students, who are overwhelmingly full-time like you and come from varied but often higher income backgrounds, your profile shows room for strengthening in core academic metrics that strongly correlate with bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where these elements predicted outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 37+ range that tips predictions favorably, while also focusing on strategies to improve your class rankings in advanced years. Building a stronger undergraduate-level record through post-bac courses or targeted GPA improvement could also help recalibrate future applications. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these key areas, and reapplying after targeted preparation often leads to better results. You're capable of closing these gaps with focused effort.
